title: CrowdStrike Behavioral IOC Trending
spl:
index=endpoint sourcetype="crowdstrike:ioc"
| timechart span=1d count by ioc_type
| where count>0
description: Behavioral IOC trending measures hunting hypothesis validation over time.
value: Behavioral IOC trending measures hunting hypothesis validation over time.
implementation: Track custom IOC hits: hash, domain, IP. Retire stale IOCs to reduce noise.
knownFalsePositives: CrowdStrike detections from EICAR test files, security training samples, or red team campaigns; review prevention policy and host criticality before paging.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `TA-crowdstrike-falcon`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=crowdstrike:ioc`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Track custom IOC hits: hash, domain, IP. Retire stale IOCs to reduce noise.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=endpoint sourcetype="crowdstrike:ioc"
| timechart span=1d count by ioc_type
| where count>0
```

Understanding this SPL

**CrowdStrike Behavioral IOC Trending** — Behavioral IOC trending measures hunting hypothesis validation over time.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=crowdstrike:ioc`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `TA-crowdstrike-falcon`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: endpoint; **sourcetype**: crowdstrike:ioc. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Detection type** for this use case: Hunting — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=endpoint, sourcetype="crowdstrike:ioc". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1d** buckets with a separate series **by ioc_type** — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.
• Filters the current rows with `where count>0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search over a window that includes both normal production traffic and, if you have one, a documented test (vendor sandbox, lab rule hit, or exercise). Compare a few sample events to the vendor’s own console or reports and tune indexes, fields, and thresholds so alerts are actionable before paging.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (IOC hits), Table (top values).
grandmaExplanation: We use CrowdStrike data and alerts to stay ahead of the risks described in "CrowdStrike Behavioral IOC Trending". We help the team notice trouble early, while a situation is still small and containable.
dataModelAcceleration: After vendor logs are CIM-tagged, enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM models listed on this use case; otherwise the optional tstats path may return no rows.
